title: Graphing Linear Equations by Plotting Points

epigraph.text:
I have set my rainbow in the clouds, and it will be 
the sign of the covenant between me and the earth.
epigraph.quote: A picture is worth a thousand words.

content.text: {A picture is worth a thousand words.}{Frederick R.\ Barnard}

An equation in two variables, such as $y = 2x + 3$, has infinitely many 
solutions—each one an ordered pair $(x, y)$ that makes the equation 
true. When we plot these solutions, a remarkable thing happens: they 
form a picture. For linear equations, that picture is always a 
straight line.
Solutions of Equations in Two Variables

content.title: Solution of an Equation in Two Variables
content.content:
A **solution** of an 
equation in two variables is an ordered pair $(x, y)$ that makes the 
equation true when the values are substituted.

content.title: Verifying solutions
content.problem:
Determine whether each ordered pair is a solution of $3x - 2y = 12$.

1. $(4, 0)$

2. $(2, 3)$

3. $(6, 3)$
content.solution:
Substitute each pair into the equation:

1. $3(4) - 2(0) = 12 - 0 = 12$ Yes, $(4, 0)$ is a solution.

2. $3(2) - 2(3) = 6 - 6 = 0 \neq 12$ No, $(2, 3)$ is not a solution.

3. $3(6) - 2(3) = 18 - 6 = 12$ Yes, $(6, 3)$ is a solution.

content.text:
To graph an equation, we find several solutions, plot them as points, 
and connect them. For linear equations, two points are theoretically 
sufficient (since two points determine a line), but plotting three or 
more provides a check for accuracy.

content.title: Graphing by Plotting Points
content.content:
1. Choose several values for $x$ (include negative, zero, and 
 positive values).

2. Calculate the corresponding $y$-values using the equation.

3. Write the solutions as ordered pairs.

4. Plot the points on a coordinate plane.

5. Draw a line through the points.

content.text:
Two special points on any graph are particularly important: the points 
where the graph crosses the axes.

content.title: Intercepts
content.content:
- The **$x$-intercept** is the 
 point where the graph crosses the $x$-axis. It has the form $(a, 0)$. 
 Find it by setting $y = 0$ and solving for $x$.

- The **$y$-intercept** is the 
 point where the graph crosses the $y$-axis. It has the form $(0, b)$. 
 Find it by setting $x = 0$ and solving for $y$.

content.title: Finding intercepts
content.solution:
**$x$-intercept:** Set $y = 0$.
\[
 4x + 3(0) = 12 \implies 4x = 12 \implies x = 3
\]
The $x$-intercept is $(3, 0)$.

**$y$-intercept:** Set $x = 0$.
\[
 4(0) + 3y = 12 \implies 3y = 12 \implies y = 4
\]
The $y$-intercept is $(0, 4)$.

content.text:
For equations in standard form $Ax + By = C$, graphing using intercepts 
is often the fastest method.

content.title: Graphing using intercepts
Horizontal and Vertical Lines
content.content:
- A **horizontal line** has equation 
 $y = b$, where $b$ is a constant. Every point on the line has 
 $y$-coordinate equal to $b$.

- A **vertical line** has equation 
 $x = a$, where $a$ is a constant. Every point on the line has 
 $x$-coordinate equal to $a$.

content.title: Graphing horizontal and vertical lines
Vertical Lines
content.content:
Vertical lines have no $y$-intercept (unless $x = 0$) and are the only 
lines that cannot be written in the form $y = mx + b$. We will see later 
that vertical lines have undefined slope.

marginNotes.text: Show me your ways, Lord, teach me your paths.
